questions.question: Define an algorithm and state its five essential characteristics with brief explanations of each.
keyPoints: Definition: Finite sequence of well-defined instructions transforming input to output
Finiteness: Must terminate after finite number of steps
Definiteness: Each instruction must be unambiguous and precise
Input: Zero or more well-defined inputs from specified domain
Output: One or more well-defined outputs that solve the problem
Effectiveness: Each instruction must be basic enough to be carried out

questions.question: Distinguish between an algorithm, a program, and a process. Why is this distinction important in algorithm analysis?
keyPoints: Algorithm: Abstract, language-independent specification of computational procedure
Program: Concrete implementation of an algorithm in a specific programming language
Process: Runtime instance of a program executing on computer with allocated resources
Distinction important: Analysis focuses on algorithm properties (efficiency), not implementation details or execution environment

questions.question:
Explain why each of the following is NOT an algorithm:
(a) A procedure containing an infinite loop
(b) An instruction 'Compute the largest prime number'
(c) The instruction 'Sort the array somehow'
keyPoints: (a) Violates finiteness - never terminates, no output produced
(b) Violates effectiveness - 'largest prime' doesn't exist, infinite computation impossible
(c) Violates definiteness - 'somehow' is ambiguous, multiple interpretations possible

questions.question: What is pseudocode? How does it differ from an algorithm? Illustrate with an example.
keyPoints: Pseudocode: Textual representation mixing natural language with programming constructs
Algorithm: The logical sequence of steps (the 'idea'); pseudocode is a notation to express it
Example: Algorithm to find max: Pseudocode shows structure; algorithm is the concept of comparing elements

questions.question: Prove or disprove: Every algorithm can be implemented as a program in some programming language.
keyPoints: Prove: Church-Turing thesis states any effectively computable function has an algorithm
Any algorithm (as finite, definite, effective procedure) can be expressed as Turing machine computation
Since any Turing machine can be simulated by programs in C/Python/Java, every algorithm is implementable
Caveat: Only well-defined algorithms with computable operations are implementable

questions.question: Discuss the historical origin of the term 'algorithm' and its evolution to modern computer science.
keyPoints: Origin: Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi, 9th-century Persian mathematician
His work 'Kitab al-Jabr' introduced Hindu-Arabic numerals and algebraic methods to Europe
Latin 'algorismus' (corruption of al-Khwarizmi) evolved to 'algorithm'
Initially meant 'arithmetic with Hindu-Arabic numerals'; now means systematic computational procedure

questions.question: Why is 'definiteness' considered fundamental to algorithm execution? What problems arise from ambiguous instructions?
keyPoints: Definiteness ensures each instruction has exactly one interpretation
Without definiteness, same 'algorithm' may produce different results on different executions or different computers
This makes algorithm behavior unpredictable and non-reproducible
In practice: compiler errors, undefined behavior, race conditions all stem from violation of definiteness
Essential for correctness proof and complexity analysis—need predictable execution paths

questions.question:
Consider the following procedure P:
1. Input: A positive integer n
2. Set result = 1
3. For i from 1 to n, multiply result by i
4. Output result and stop

(a) Identify what problem this algorithm solves
(b) Analyze which of the five algorithm characteristics are satisfied
(c) How would you express this algorithm in mathematical notation?
(d) What is the time complexity of this algorithm in terms of input size?
keyPoints: (a) Computes n! (factorial of n)
(b) Finiteness: Yes (loop runs n times then stops), Definiteness: Yes (each step precise), Input: Yes (positive integer n), Output: Yes (result), Effectiveness: Yes (basic arithmetic operations)
(c) f(n) = n! = ∏(i=1 to n) i, or f: ℕ → ℕ where f(n) = n!
(d) Input size = n (the value itself), time complexity = O(n) for the loop; mathematically, number of primitive operations is Θ(n)

questions.question:
Develop a formal framework for defining and classifying computational procedures as algorithms. Your framework should:
(a) Provide a formal mathematical definition of an algorithm using set theory or logic
(b) Define what constitutes a 'valid' algorithm versus an 'invalid' computational procedure
(c) Show how your framework can determine whether a given procedure qualifies as an algorithm
(d) Discuss how this framework connects to algorithm analysis (time/space complexity)
keyPoints: (a) Formal definition: Algorithm A is a tuple (I, O, S, s₀, s*, δ, λ) where I=inputs, O=outputs, S=states, s₀=initial, s*=halt, δ=transition function, λ=output function. Each step must be from finite instruction set, execution deterministic.

(b) Valid algorithm: All five characteristics satisfied (prove using framework). Invalid: fails any—non-terminating (violates finiteness), ambiguous instruction (violates definiteness), impossible operation (violates effectiveness).
(c) Given procedure P, check: (i) Finite instruction set? (ii) Unique next state for each state? (iii) Halting state reachable? (iv) Output function defined for halting states? All yes → algorithm.

(d) Framework enables complexity analysis: count primitive operations in δ, measure state space |S| for space, analyze transition sequence length for time. Asymptotic notations (sibling topic) provide bounds on these measures.

questions.question: Compare and contrast algorithms with heuristics. In scenarios where optimal solutions are computationally infeasible, when would you choose an algorithm versus a heuristic? Provide examples from sorting and shortest path problems.
keyPoints: Algorithm: Guarantees correct/optimal solution (if exists) through deterministic procedure. Heuristic: Approximate solution without guarantee of correctness, usually faster.
Algorithm: Sorting—merge sort guarantees O(n log n), correct order. Heuristic—insertion sort faster on nearly sorted data but not optimal in worst case.
Algorithm: Shortest path—Dijkstra guarantees shortest path. Heuristic—A* uses heuristic estimates for faster search in specific cases.

Choice criteria: (i) Is optimal solution required? (ii) What is input size? (iii) Time constraints? (iv) Is problem NP-hard? For large NP-hard problems, heuristics often necessary—traveling salesman uses nearest-neighbor heuristic.
Trade-off: Algorithm provides theoretical guarantee; heuristic provides practical efficiency with acceptable approximation.
